source_inputs.use_in_this_artifact: business objective, audience, public-demo constraint, and deck boundary
product capability ids, causal product logic ids, and forbidden empty claims
reader questions, required product terms, and proof obligations
public proof objects, copy routes, and note/viewer boundaries
source-quality limits and release-blocking evidence constraints
tutorial-derived renderer action lineage used only as design-move evidence

artifact_scope.goal: Define the product claim graph before writing slide story or scene compiler structures.
artifact_scope.quality_gate: Every product claim must identify capability ids, causal logic ids, proof objects, user benefit, visible result, and public-surface routing.

product_definition.one_sentence: Vulca turns a commercial brief plus curated source memory into an editable PPT product demo whose claims, design moves, proof objects, and release gates can be inspected.
product_definition.business_objective: Make the PPT wedge understandable, credible, and visually strong enough for a product video without claiming public release readiness.

public_surface_routing.on_canvas_policy: Use plain product language for input, mechanism, editable output, user benefit, and visible proof object.
public_surface_routing.reason: The source contracts require these obligations, but the public slide surface should not become a raw trace or schema report.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: Vulca is a source-bound system for turning a real brief into editable PPT.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: The buyer can understand the product in one sentence before judging the visuals.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: A commercial brief, source database, workflow path, editable PPT preview, and review boundary are visible as one product path.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: The claim is credible only if the output is described as editable PPT and the review boundary remains visible.

speaker_note_or_viewer: The source contract also carries raw trace and review metadata.
Do not describe the product as image-only generation.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: The system starts from a brief and allowed source memory, not from a blank prompt.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: A reviewer can separate legitimate source-derived guidance from copied source visuals or generic prompt output.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: The deck can show source pack, source boundary, and selected memory without exposing copied source surfaces.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: Only source records with usable derived signals can drive content or design decisions.

speaker_note_or_viewer: Source quality audit records decide which inputs are generator-ready.
Missing decomposition stays blocked from generation.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: Source observations become product memory, message obligations, and proof objects before drawing.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: The team can explain why each product claim exists and what evidence it must carry.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: A source-to-memory compiler stage with selected source pack, capability memory, and public proof object.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: The compiler must preserve reader questions, required terms, and proof routes without putting raw field lists on the slide.

speaker_note_or_viewer: Reader questions and raw trace obligations remain inspectable in the viewer.
Weak or generic claims become repair inputs.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: Tutorial principles become renderer decisions only after they are mapped to PPT actions.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: The deck can feel designed while remaining controlled by explicit renderer actions.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: The viewer sees hierarchy, spacing, build rhythm, and causal motion as product behavior rather than decoration.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: Each design move must trace back to tutorial, visual target, and motion target ids from B2.

on_canvas: Tutorial rule mapped to PPT action
Hierarchy, spacing, rhythm, and causal reading flow
speaker_note_or_viewer: The full renderer action list remains in the B2 artifact.
This graph does not start scene compiler geometry.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: The output is editable native PPT, not a full-slide image pretending to be a deck.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: A team can inspect, edit, and hand off the generated deck instead of accepting a raster mockup.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: An editable PPT preview owns the product moment and carries attached proof tags.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: The public claim must name editable PPT output and avoid implying the final deck is image-only.

speaker_note_or_viewer: Native object and layout checks remain review artifacts.
Images may support atmosphere but cannot replace editable structure.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: Trust comes from inspectable proof and bad-control boundaries, not from a nicer-looking slide.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: Reviewers can audit whether the workflow consumed the content layer and why a bad control fails.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: A compact inspection board shows capability binding, contract match, content check, and bad-control failure.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: Raw proof fields must be preserved for review while public copy stays compact.

speaker_note_or_viewer: Trace field, content workflow gate, reader question, and required term obligations remain inspectable off-canvas.
The bad control and full proof records are not public-surface copy.

product_claim_nodes.public_claim: The next proof must be generated and reviewed before public release is claimed.
product_claim_nodes.user_benefit: The team gets a credible handoff that separates content readiness from release readiness.
product_claim_nodes.visible_user_result: A release decision wall shows content layer, next generated proof, human review, source-boundary review, and blocked release.
product_claim_nodes.proof_standard: The graph can feed C2, but it cannot claim public readiness or start rendering.

speaker_note_or_viewer: Run-specific content-layer and generated-proof terms stay in speaker notes or viewer trace.
The next generated run must consume this graph before native drawing.

causal_edges.causal_relation: A source-bound product identity requires a clear boundary between allowed source memory and copied source surfaces.
causal_edges.business_logic: Without the boundary, the product looks like prompt creativity or source imitation rather than controlled generation.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: The buyer can trust the starting point of the workflow.

causal_edges.causal_relation: Allowed source memory is compiled into product claims, reader questions, and proof objects before drawing.
causal_edges.business_logic: The deck can explain each claim because the claim exists before the renderer chooses visual structure.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: The buyer sees a repeatable reasoning path instead of a finished slide with no provenance.

causal_edges.causal_relation: A product claim selects what must be emphasized; B2 design moves decide how PPT objects should express that emphasis.
causal_edges.business_logic: The design layer cannot be free-form decoration; it must serve the product claim.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: The viewer gets clearer hierarchy and less decorative noise.

causal_edges.causal_relation: Mapped design moves are converted into native PPT structure instead of raster composition.
causal_edges.business_logic: The product value appears only when the result remains editable and inspectable.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: The team can reuse, inspect, and hand off the generated deck.

causal_edges.causal_relation: Native output is only credible when proof, comparison, and verification records remain attached.
causal_edges.business_logic: A nice deck without inspectable proof cannot demonstrate product advantage.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: Reviewers can decide whether the product actually used the workflow.

causal_edges.causal_relation: Failed or incomplete verification becomes the next content and render contract rather than a public claim.
causal_edges.business_logic: The release boundary protects credibility while preserving a clear next action.
causal_edges.user_value_delta: The team knows what proof must be generated next before publication.

why_credible: The product starts from a commercial case and allowed source memory rather than a blank prompt.
Capability ids and logic relation ids preserve the product mechanism behind each claim.
Narrative proof ids preserve public proof objects without putting raw trace on the slide.
B2 tutorial-to-renderer mappings keep design moves executable instead of ornamental.
The source audit blocks weak source records from direct generation.
failure_boundaries: Do not claim source evidence that lacks extracted design signal.
Do not render copied source visuals, source brands, or full transcripts.
Do not treat this graph as a slide story or scene compiler.
Do not claim public release from content logic alone.
trust_model.release_truth: public release remains blocked until review passes

what_user_sees: a real brief entering the system
allowed source memory becoming product claims
tutorial principles binding to PPT actions
editable PPT output with attached proof
a release gate that names the next proof
what_user_can_do: see what should be visible on the public slide
route raw proof details to notes or viewer
decide whether the next renderer run is ready
user_result_model.business_value: The deck story can now sell a controllable product workflow rather than a sequence of attractive but hollow slides.
